transcript.clean_text: The 2020 edition of the Flèche Wallonne would be a different one like all races this year. An autumn classic instead of a spring classic, but the same finish atop the mighty Mur de Huy. Three laps of a finishing circuit of 32 km with a brand new couple of climbs in before the finish. Four riders went away in the first 30 K's of the day. Headline name among them, the man wearing number seven, 21-year-old Mauri Van Sevenant, making his debut on the World Tour. Chasing behind Sunweb, UAE Emirates, and the Ineos Grenadiers. Sunweb having Marc Hirschi as their leader. Michał Kwiatkowski doing his thing for Ineos Grenadiers. In the meantime, for UAE Emirates, their leader Tadej Pogačar. Dan Martin would attack from behind alongside Schelling. And the breakaway with one lap to go would start to break up. Maximum gap of 9 minutes was down to around one with 25 km to go. EF and Mitchelton-Scott starting to join the chase. Van Sevenant would be the last survivor of the breakaway, dropping Paasschens, the Dutchman. And on the penultimate climb of the day, Rigoberto Urán dropped off in a perfect position and starting to really motor away from the peloton. Van Sevenant would have 15 seconds on Urán, just over half a minute on the peloton. And going into the final couple of kilometers, his team chasing on behind, waiting to see if he'd be caught. But then this would happen. Van Sevenant off into a ditch, his gap disappearing, and with it all chances of fighting it out for the win on the last climb. Urán would catch him, but they'd be caught right at the bottom of the Mur de Huy as the favorites all attempted to be positioned. Polanc would do his thing for Pogačar at the bottom of the climb. Sergio Henao then take it on. Dries Devenyns dropping off, but surely Poels the first of the favorites to have a go. And then it'd be up to Mike Woods to do the same. 200 m to go when he had his acceleration, trying to become the first Canadian ever to win the race. Hirschi in the white jersey on his wheel, and after a brilliant bronze at the World Championships, a stage win at the Tour de France, he waited until 50 m to go here to launch his own acceleration, and it would be the winning move. Marc Hirschi, the first Swiss rider since the great Ferdi Kübler to win this race. 1952 the last Swiss win. 2020, a Flèche Wallonne victory for the man from Bern. What form, what a moment, and what ability at 22 years of age. Marc Hirschi, the winner ahead of Benoît Cosnefroy, and in third place, Mike Woods. Barguil would be fourth, Dan Martin to a brilliant fifth with Kwiatkowski, Konrad, Poels, Pogačar, and Gesbert all making up a superbly strong top 10. Marc Hirschi though, the man of the day at Flèche Wallonne.
